senses.definition: Describing an accent that pronounces the /r/ sound wherever the letter r appears in spelling, including before consonants and at the end of words.
examples: General American English is usually described as a rhotic accent.
In rhotic varieties of English, the r in words like "car" and "hard" is clearly pronounced.
Scottish English is typically rhotic, unlike many accents in southern England.

senses.definition: Relating to an r-like consonant sound.
examples: The child had difficulty producing rhotic sounds correctly.
In phonetics, rhotic consonants form an important class of liquid sounds.

senses.definition: Different degrees or types of rhotic pronunciation in accents or dialects.
examples: The study compares the rhoticities of several regional varieties of English.
Sociolinguists have described changing rhoticities in urban speech over time.

senses.definition: The presence, absence, or degree of /r/ pronunciation in a particular accent or dialect.
examples: Rhoticity is one of the main features used to classify varieties of English.
Recent research shows a decline in rhoticity among younger speakers in that region.
The rhoticity of an accent can signal social background and identity.

senses.definition: A garden plant with large leaves and thick red or green stems that are cooked and eaten, especially in sweet dishes.
examples: She made a pie filled with fresh rhubarb and strawberries.
Rhubarb has a sharp, sour taste, so it is usually cooked with sugar.
They grow rhubarb in the corner of their vegetable garden.

senses.definition: A loud argument or angry disagreement, especially in sports contexts in North American English.
examples: The manager got into a rhubarb with the umpire over the strike call.
There was quite a rhubarb on the field after the referee’s decision.

senses.definition: Containing or covered with rhubarb, especially as an ingredient in food or drink.
examples: We shared a beautifully rhubarbed dessert at the new restaurant.
The menu featured a rhubarbed cocktail with a bright pink color.

senses.definition: The act or process of using rhubarb as an ingredient in cooking, especially in a playful or creative way.
examples: Weekend rhubarbing has become her new hobby in the kitchen.
His blog is full of enthusiastic rhubarbing and experimental recipes.

senses.definition: Various episodes or results of cooking or experimenting with rhubarb.
examples: She wrote amusing notes about her latest rhubarbings in the kitchen.
Their seasonal rhubarbings always fill the house with a sweet smell.

senses.definition: Plural of rhubarb, referring to more than one rhubarb plant or its edible stems.
examples: The garden beds are full of healthy rhubarbs this year.
She bought two fresh rhubarbs at the market.

senses.definition: Plural of rhubarb when meaning arguments or noisy disagreements.
examples: There were several rhubarbs during the tense match.
Fans will remember the famous rhubarbs between those two teams.

senses.definition: Having the taste, color, or smell of rhubarb.
examples: The sauce had a pleasantly rhubarby sharpness.
She chose a dress in a soft rhubarby pink.

senses.definition: A line on the earth’s surface that cuts all meridians at the same angle, used in navigation; also called a rhumb line.
examples: The ship followed a constant rhumb across the ocean.
On some charts, a rhumb appears as a straight line for easier navigation.

senses.definition: A slow Latin American dance with pronounced hip movements, or the music for this dance.
examples: They learned the basic steps of the rhumba for their wedding dance.
The band played a lively rhumba that filled the dance floor.
She prefers rhumba to faster dances like salsa.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rhumba: danced the rhumba.
examples: They rhumbaed together under the soft lights of the ballroom.
We rhumbaed all night at the festival.

senses.definition: Present participle of rhumba: dancing the rhumba.
examples: Couples were rhumbaing happily around the room.
She spent the evening rhumbaing with her classmates.

senses.definition: Plural of rhumba, referring to more than one rhumba dance or piece of rhumba music.
examples: The band played several rhumbas during the evening.
They practiced different rhumbas for the competition.

senses.definition: A type of resonant cavity used to accelerate charged particles in early particle accelerators.
examples: The rhumbatron was a key component in some early cyclotrons.
Physicists adjusted the rhumbatron to increase the particles’ energy.

senses.definition: Plural of rhumbatron, referring to several such resonant cavities in particle accelerators.
examples: The design uses multiple rhumbatrons to speed up the beam.
Older accelerators employed rhumbatrons rather than modern structures.

senses.definition: Plural of rhumb, referring to several rhumb lines used in navigation.
examples: The chart shows different rhumbs radiating from the port.
Navigators once relied heavily on rhumbs for long sea journeys.

senses.definition: A plant of the genus Rhus, such as sumac, some species of which can cause skin irritation.
examples: She developed a rash after touching a rhus in the garden.
Certain rhus species are grown for their bright autumn leaves.

senses.definition: Plural of rhus, referring to several plants of the genus Rhus.
examples: The hillside was dotted with colorful rhuses in autumn.
Some rhuses are used ornamentally, while others are avoided as irritants.

senses.definition: A shortened or dialect form sometimes used for "rhyming" or "rhyme", mainly in non-standard or creative writing.
examples: The poet played with rhy and rhythm throughout the piece.
In the song’s lyrics, "rhy" is used as a playful spelling.

senses.definition: A rare mineral related to certain volcanic rocks, mentioned mainly in geological writing.
examples: Samples of rhyacolite were collected from the ancient lava flow.
The presence of rhyacolite suggests a particular cooling history.

senses.definition: A word that has the same ending sound as another word, or the use of such words in poetry and songs.
examples: "Cat" and "hat" are a simple rhyme.
Children enjoy songs with lots of rhyme and rhythm.
He looked for a rhyme for "orange" but could not find one.

senses.definition: To have the same ending sound as another word, or to use such words when writing poetry.
examples: "Play" and "day" rhyme with each other.
She loves to rhyme words when she writes lyrics.
The last lines of the poem do not rhyme.

senses.definition: Past simple and past participle of rhyme.
examples: The lines rhymed neatly at the ends.
As a child, he rhymed words just for fun.

senses.definition: Written or arranged with lines that end in the same or similar sounds.
examples: He prefers rhymed verse to free verse.
The song is a short, rhymed piece for children.

senses.definition: Without any rhymes, or difficult to find a rhyme for.
examples: Some people consider "silver" to be a rhymeless word.
He chose a rhymeless structure to give the poem a more natural sound.

senses.definition: A very short or simple rhyme, often light or playful.
examples: She wrote a little rhymelet for her niece’s birthday card.
The book is full of rhymelets for early readers.

senses.definition: Someone who writes rhymes, especially in a simple or playful way.
examples: As a child, he was a natural rhymemaker.
Many songwriters are skilled rhymemakers as well as musicians.

senses.definition: The activity or skill of creating rhymes.
examples: Rhymemaking can help learners remember new words.
His approach to rhymemaking mixes humor with deep emotion.

senses.definition: Very difficult or impossible to find a true rhyme for.
examples: Poets often call "month" a rhymeproof word.
He picked a rhymeproof name to avoid cheesy slogans.

senses.definition: Someone who writes or recites rhymes.
examples: He became a popular rhymer at local poetry events.
Children love listening to a good rhymer perform.

senses.definition: Plural of rhymer, referring to several people who write or recite rhymes.
examples: The event brought together rhymers from across the city.
Modern rhymers often mix spoken word with music.

senses.definition: The art, practice, or style of making rhymes.
examples: The novel is full of playful rhymery and word games.
His early work shows a love of simple rhymery.

senses.definition: Plural of rhyme, referring to several words or pieces of writing with similar end sounds.
examples: The teacher asked the class to find three rhymes for "light".
Nursery rhymes are often the first songs children learn.

senses.definition: Third person singular of rhyme.
examples: The last word almost rhymes with "home".
Her name rhymes with a common English word.

senses.definition: Someone who writes light, simple, or not very serious verse, often seen as not a very good poet.
examples: Critics dismissed him as a mere rhymester.
The magazine sometimes prints verses by amateur rhymesters.

senses.definition: Plural of rhymester, referring to several writers of light or inferior verse.
examples: He liked to joke that all great poets begin as rhymesters.
The city’s streets are full of young rhymesters performing their work.

senses.definition: In terms of rhyme or regarding rhyme.
examples: Rhymewise, the song is quite simple.
Rhymewise, those two lines do not match at all.

senses.definition: Non-standard spelling sometimes used for "rhythmic" or "rhyming", especially in informal or creative contexts.
examples: The track has a strong, rhymic beat.
His rhymic style makes the poem easy to remember.

senses.definition: Having the same or a similar ending sound.
examples: Children often enjoy simple rhyming words.
She made a list of rhyming pairs to help her write lyrics.

senses.definition: The activity or process of making words or lines that rhyme.
examples: Rhyming is a useful way to remember vocabulary.
He has a talent for fast rhyming in freestyle rap.

senses.definition: A person who is skilled at writing rhymes.
examples: He is known as a clever rhymist in the local poetry scene.
The songwriter is a gifted rhymist as well as a strong singer.

senses.definition: Plural of rhymist, referring to several people skilled at writing rhymes.
examples: The festival features rhymists from around the world.
Young rhymists are changing the sound of contemporary poetry.

senses.definition: Full of rhymes or using a lot of rhymes.
examples: The children enjoyed the book’s playful, rhymy text.
Her early songs are simple and very rhymy.

senses.definition: An order or group of leeches that have a protrusible proboscis and lack jaws.
examples: Rhynchobdellae are characterized by an extendable proboscis used for feeding.
The researcher specializes in the study of Rhynchobdellae.

senses.definition: An order of leeches that have an extendable proboscis, no jaws, and colorless blood.
examples: Rhynchobdellida includes several species that parasitize fish.
The anatomy of Rhynchobdellida makes them distinct from jawed leeches.

senses.definition: A variant or historical name related to Rhynchocephalia, an order of ancient lizard-like reptiles.
examples: Fossils of rhynchocephala have been found in Mesozoic rocks.
The term rhynchocephala appears in older zoological literature.

senses.definition: Plural or variant form connected with Rhynchocephalia, referring to reptiles of that group.
examples: The museum displays several rhynchocephali from different periods.
Researchers compare modern tuataras with fossil rhynchocephali.

senses.definition: An order of lizard-like reptiles, mostly extinct, whose only living member is the tuatara.
examples: Rhynchocephalia were common in the Mesozoic era.
The tuatara is the only surviving representative of Rhynchocephalia.

senses.definition: Relating to the order Rhynchocephalia of lizard-like reptiles.
examples: The skull displays several rhynchocephalian features.
This fossil is believed to belong to a rhynchocephalian species.

senses.definition: A reptile belonging to the order Rhynchocephalia.
examples: The tuatara is the best-known living rhynchocephalian.
Many rhynchocephalians died out by the end of the Jurassic period.

senses.definition: Plural of rhynchocephalian, referring to several reptiles in the order Rhynchocephalia.
examples: Most rhynchocephalians became extinct millions of years ago.
Scientists study rhynchocephalians to understand early reptile evolution.

senses.definition: Having characteristics of or relating to Rhynchocephalia or rhynchocephalians.
examples: The fossil shows a distinctly rhynchocephalic skull.
These rhynchocephalic traits separate the group from true lizards.

senses.definition: Describing an animal that belongs to or resembles the Rhynchocephalia.
examples: Rhynchocephalous reptiles were once widespread.
The creature’s rhynchocephalous form puzzled early researchers.

senses.definition: A fluid-filled cavity in some invertebrates, especially ribbon worms, that houses and supports the proboscis.
examples: The rhynchocoel acts as a hydrostatic skeleton for the proboscis.
In nemerteans, the rhynchocoel runs almost the entire length of the body.
